text: Welcome to another episode of This is My Architecture. Today I have here with me
Tom from T-Mobile. Hi Tom. Hi. So Tom, we all know T-Mobile, so tell me a little bit of what your team does.
I work in a team that does central planning for cloud. We have hundreds of teams, thousands of
developers at T-Mobile, and many of those teams use the public cloud, use AWS. And so what we do is we build
platforms, security tools, and other services to enable those teams to build our customer-facing features on AWS.
I see. And those tools that you build for them, do you have a standardized way of deploying them?
Yeah. So we will build, for example, our Kubernetes infrastructure. We have a standard way of building
Kubernetes infrastructure where we will use our automation tools like Terraform and Ansible.
And basically what we're building when we do that is we're building Kubernetes clusters
with making sure we have our identity management baked into the clusters we're building.
We're building and the 20 to 30 different components that go into building a Kubernetes cluster.
And what this does, so this is the automation stack that we use. What this does is it allows us to have
a standard way to build Kubernetes clusters across the entire organization. So anyone who's consuming
Kubernetes is doing the same thing. We don't have drift in terms of the number of different components
people are using the versions or the state, that sort of thing.
Okay. So basically they can just take what you build and deploy as many times as they want,
depending on what applications they are developing.
Correct. We have several applications. For example, our e-commerce application, our retail applications,
our internet service provider applications that use this system with hundreds of applications that use
this system. And they're all deploying to a standard infrastructure across all the applications.
Okay. Let's dive a little bit deeper into the architecture of this standard infrastructure.
So you mentioned Kubernetes. What else are these teams using?
So the ability to build these common clusters allows our team to focus on the infrastructure,
allows the application teams to be able to consume things like RDS, for example.
Mm-hmm.
So teams don't have to spend the time on thinking about what infrastructure they're building.
We're using a GitOps model, for example. And so any change to the state of any cluster anywhere,
when there's 40 clusters now running 30,000 containers across hundreds of applications,
any change to any of those is done through a GitOps process, which is just a MergeQuest, for example.
And so that has a review process and it goes out to production or non-prog.
I see. Okay. So the infrastructure deployment is completely automated through GitHub and Terraform.
And the infrastructure itself, do they have the same components? Each team uses the same components in the
infrastructure? Or do you have different components depending on the needs of this team?
So from a component point of view, what we're trying to do is we're trying to build,
effectively, we're trying to get to the point where we have a mutable infrastructure.
And that's really the goal. So we've been at this a long time. We've been doing this for
almost five years now, where we've had large-scale container-based environments. And we've evolved to
the point now where we want to basically just say that a cluster is not something that's living,
that you deploy to it when there's configuration change needed. You remove it and redeploy through
this GitOps process to deliver immutable infrastructure. So this is kind of the end state
of where we want to be. This enables us, for example, to take all these applications that we have. Like,
for example, each of these applications lives in a VPC. This VPC will span East and West regions.
So we get, you know, HA out of this. And so this is really when we want to make sure that everything
is consistent across these. We'll also have prod versions of these and non-prod versions of dev
versions of these as well. So that we can have consistency in every single environment that someone
uses. We'll also make sure that we can have a single way to build clusters, even though we have
different needs for different clusters. We have dedicated clusters for teams. We have shared, where
many teams are in one cluster. And so we have clusters that span accounts or clusters that span VPCs. And
all these things need to be baked into how we deliver our code for our infrastructure.
And all of this, let's say, dedicated share accounts, do they share information at any time?
Are they connected to these VPCs at any time? Yeah, everything is done through
Transit Gateway here. So we have a model where we have about 100 accounts or so, which is a fairly
small number for a company our size and the scale we're at. But we're able to do that because of tools
like Transit Gateway. And Transit Gateway allows us to connect into our data centers. And so we have
data centers in Texas and Washington State. And so that allows all these applications here to connect
back to any information they need from the data centers. And so we can replicate this process across
many different accounts, many different use cases.
So you're deploying this across your data center, regions. Are you deploying also closer to your users?
Yeah. So one example, one good benefit of having a model like this is you can take something new,
like a local zone, for example. And we can use exactly the same process to deliver out to now the LA local zone.
So in LA, you have a new local zone that we can deliver to. And that has two AZs. And all these
clusters we built all span three AZs. And so we want to be able to do the same thing here where you have
an A and a B on LAX and deliver this. So we were able to do this. Now, a local zone is different than a region.
And so you have to adjust for that. But we were able to make those adjustments and deliver
a local zone within a week. So it was pretty straightforward for us to be able to deliver that.
So what was the reason to actually see the deployment on a local zone? Do you have any
applications that need single second latency? Yeah. So as a telecom, we really understand the need for
low latency and to build applications where customers can access things quicker. But on
Amazon today, we use West and East. So we need more options. We need to be able to service our
customers in LA, Florida, but they're not exactly close to a region. And so one of the ways we can do
that is through local zones. So if you can think of, for example, a store locator application where
every time somebody is looking for a store, they're in pretty much a local, they're looking for something
near to them. So that will help us make that a quicker process. So you already deployed on local
zones. Was there any difference? Was there any improvement on the latency when you did that?
Yeah, it was significant. So we've been measuring the difference between local zones. So a user in LA
going to a local zone will experience pretty low latency in the area of five milliseconds as what
we've tested. But going back to the to the AZ will be something like 50 milliseconds. So it's an order
of magnitude faster. I see. And just a last question that I have for you is all of this infrastructure,
I'm assuming that you are, besides the automation, also constantly monitoring it. Yeah. So obviously,
we hook into CloudWatch, we pull of our CloudWatch into this infrastructure as well through Prometheus.
And we have observability is one of the key points to being able to do this at all,
never mind doing it at scale. And so this is something we really leverage very, make sure that we
have all the alerts firing and we know the state of our applications, our infrastructure across AWS and
across all of our clusters. This is very interesting. Thank you so much for sharing this with us. Sure.
Thank you. And thank you for watching another episode of This is My Architecture.
